review_summary: Transcript is sufficient for reinforcement questions about L2 regularization, the modern view of generalization, and how dropout behaves in training versus test time. No separate clip is needed.

question_bank.question: What does L2 regularization add to the training objective?
choices: A reward for using larger weights
A squared-parameter penalty that favors smaller weights
A rule that deletes random hidden units
A requirement that training loss reach zero
question_bank.explanation: L2 regularization augments the loss with a penalty on parameter magnitude, which biases learning toward smaller weights.

question_bank.question: Why does the segment say the old textbook picture of overfitting is incomplete for modern deep learning?
choices: Modern models never overfit at all
Modern networks are too small to memorize data
Very large models can fit the training set extremely well and still generalize with good regularization
Validation loss is no longer measured in deep learning
question_bank.explanation: The segment argues that large networks can nearly memorize training data yet still generalize if the learning setup and regularization are good.

transcript_quotes: you can essentially train them to get 0 loss

question_bank.question: What happens during dropout training?
choices: All hidden units are kept and gradients are averaged
A random mask zeros part of an internal representation
The vocabulary is randomly reduced each epoch
The output layer is replaced by nearest-neighbor search
question_bank.explanation: Dropout multiplies hidden representations by a random binary mask during training so the model cannot rely on the same features every time.

transcript_quotes: random mask of zeros and ones

question_bank.question: How is dropout handled at test time in the version emphasized here?
choices: Units are still randomly removed on every forward pass
All units stay active and activations are rescaled
The hidden state is reset to zeros
The model switches from SGD to Adam
question_bank.explanation: At test time the network keeps all units instead of randomly dropping them, and rescales activations to match training-time expectations.

transcript_quotes: At test time dropout keeps all units

question_bank.question: What is one intuition for why dropout can improve generalization?
choices: It forces every feature to be useful without depending too much on specific partners
It guarantees the model will use fewer parameters overall
It prevents gradients from ever becoming noisy
It makes every hidden unit represent the same feature
question_bank.explanation: By randomly removing features during training, dropout discourages fragile co-adaptation and pushes the model toward more robust representations.
